does: This paper introduces BEAP, a black-box adversarial prompting attack against unlearned text-to-image diffusion models. BEAP uses a large language model to iteratively generate natural-language prompts that cause supposedly unlearned concepts to be generated despite removal efforts. The method performs embedding-aware search in text space, optimizing for three objectives simultaneously: presence of the unlearned concept in generated images, text-image alignment quality, and overall image quality. Unlike prior attacks, BEAP generates adversarial prompts that are grammatically natural and semantically coherent, evading simple rule-based safety filters while achieving high attack success rates without requiring access to model weights.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of attacks against machine unlearning in generative models. It responds to limitations in prior white-box and black-box attack methods that either required model weight access or produced easily-detectable gibberish prompts. The paper builds on research in adversarial prompting and jailbreaking of large language models by repurposing LLMs as iterative prompt optimizers. It also connects to work on understanding embedding spaces in diffusion models and exploiting their alignment properties. The research addresses a critical gap between theoretical unlearning claims and practical robustness, paralleling concurrent work on adversarial robustness in machine learning that questions whether concepts truly removed from models remain exploitable through clever input design.
enables: This work exposes fundamental challenges in achieving robust concept removal from generative models, suggesting future research must develop unlearning methods resilient to sophisticated embedding-aware attacks. It points toward the need for more comprehensive threat modeling in unlearning research that accounts for adaptive adversaries with only black-box access. The methodology could extend to attacking other conditional generation models and multimodal systems. Future work might explore defenses combining prompt filtering with deeper architectural changes, or develop certified unlearning approaches with formal guarantees against such attacks. The embedding-aware optimization framework could also inform better evaluation protocols for unlearning, establishing adversarial benchmarks that distinguish genuine concept removal from superficial suppression.
